doc.title: Weighted-average shares outstanding
doc.event: The denominator of every EPS number: 100,000 shares on Jan 1, 20,000 more issued Apr 1, and a 2-for-1 split on Jul 1. Issuances count from their date forward — but splits and stock dividends restate the clock ALL the way back.

variants.label: Issuance weighted by time; split restated retroactively

computationPaths.narration: Two different rules working the same problem. New-share issuances (Apr 1) weight by months outstanding — they only earned for part of the year. But the 2-for-1 split (Jul 1) restates EVERY earlier share count as if it had always been split — a split changes the unit of measurement, not the economics, so the whole timeline converts to post-split units.

memorize.body: WASO = Σ (shares outstanding × months ÷ 12), with splits and stock dividends restated retroactively to the START of the year (and to all comparative prior years).

Money events (issuances, buybacks) weight by time — new capital only works part of the year. Paper events (splits, stock dividends) restate all of time — no new capital, just smaller slices of the same pie.

Treasury purchases weight from their date too — REDUCING shares for the remaining months. And a split occurring after year-end but before statements issue STILL restates the year. Exams love that one.

questions.prompt: 100,000 shares Jan 1; 20,000 issued Apr 1; 2-for-1 split Jul 1. Weighted-average shares for the year =

distractors.feedback: That weights the split by time like an issuance (only Jul–Dec doubled). A split restates RETROACTIVELY — the Jan–Jun share counts double too: (200,000 × 3/12) + (240,000 × 3/12) + (240,000 × 6/12) = 230,000.
240,000 is the year-END share count. EPS needs the weighted AVERAGE — the April issuance only worked 9 months of the year.
